claims.claim: On a controlled synthetic benchmark with documents of six chunks drawn from latent topic trajectories, a bilinear scorer trained with an InfoNCE-style cross-entropy objective recovers the exact permutation in 97.4% of held-out documents (mean across five seeds), with directed adjacency accuracy of 99.47% and Kendall's τ of 0.976.

evidence_refs.quote: We investigate whether a contrastive next-chunk ordering loss can learn a directional adjacency signal from shuffled text chunks, and whether it correctly fails when no such signal exists. On a controlled synthetic benchmark with documents of six chunks drawn from latent topic trajectories, a bilinear scorer trained with an InfoNCE-style cross-entropy objective recovers the exact permutation in 97.4% of held-out documents (mean across five seeds), with directed adjacency accuracy of 99.47% and K

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: For each true adjacent pair (x_t, x_{t+1}) in a document, the loss treats x_{t+1} as the positive and all other chunks in the same document as negatives.

evidence_refs.quote: # Contrastive Chunk Ordering Loss: A Controlled Synthetic Study of Directional Next-Chunk Prediction

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: The InfoNCE-style cross-entropy objective is: $$\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{L-1} \sum_{t=1}^{L-1} \log \frac{\exp(s(x_t, x_{t+1}))}{\sum_{j \neq t} \exp(s(x_t, x_j))}$$ With *L* = 6, each positive is classified against 5 negatives, giving a chance loss of ln(5) ≈ 1.609.
